refsection.title: Purpose
refsection.para: The goal of this page is to present the Faure sequence.

refsection.title: Principles
refsection.para: Let s be the number of dimensions. The Faure sequence uses the smallest prime number greater or equal than b. For example, if s=6, then the prime number b used by the Faure sequence is 7.
Let k be an integer and let a_i(k) be the coefficients of the base-b decomposition of k:

Let i be an integer in the set {1,2,...,s}. The i-th coordinate of the Faure sequence makes use of a r-by-r upper triangular matrix, called the generator matrix. For the Faure sequence, the generator matrix is based on the Pascal matrix defined as:
where
for n\geq m and zero otherwise.
By convention C^{(0)} is identity matrix.
Consider the column vector a(k) defined by ordering the digits from the least significant to the most significant:
We introduce the column vector y(k) defined by the matrix-vector product:
The i-th component of the Faure sequence is:

refsection.title: Properties
refsection.para: The matrices C^{(i)} are so that

is a permutation of the vector a(k) for the set of points k=jb^r,...,(j+1)b^r-1, for any j=0,1,...,b-1. Hence, for this set of points, each component x^{(i)}_k is a permutation of the Van Der Corput sequence \psi_b(k).
The first component of the Faure sequence is the Van Der Corput sequence in base b. Other components of the Faure sequence are permutations of sub-sequences of the Van Der Corput sequence in base b.

Faure sequences are (0,s)-sequences in base b. Therefore, Faure sequences have the lowest possible value of the quality parameter t in base b. On the other hand, the base increases with the dimension s, although the base for the Faure sequence increases much slower than the largest base of the Halton sequence.

refsection.title: Examples
refsection.para: The following function computes the Faure matrix, given the number of digits r and the dimension index i.
refsection.programlisting: function c = faurematrix ( r , i ) c = zeros(r,r) for m = 1:r b=specfun_nchoosek ((m:r)-1 , m-1) c(m,m:r) = i.^((m:r)-m) .* b end endfunction
refsection.para: For r=1, the Faure matrix is equal to one. The following script prints the Faure matrices for increasing values of r and i.
refsection.programlisting: for r=2:3 for i=1:3 c = faurematrix ( r , i ); mprintf("r=%d, C(%d)\n",r,i) disp(c) end end
refsection.para: The previous script produces the following output.

The following script produces the first 10 points of the Faure sequence in s=3 dimensions. For this value of s, the Faure sequence uses the base b=3. We use the number_tobary function in order to decompose the integer k into base b. The "bigendian" option reverses the order of the digits, so that the most significant digit comes last. Then we compute the matrix-vector product C*a and compute the remainder modulo b. Finally, we evaluate the point by using powers of the inverse of b.
The previous script produces the following output.
We can compare with the second component of the Halton sequence in s=2 dimensions, which uses the prime number b=3 as the base.
The previous script produces the following output.

The following script computes the elements of the first 27 points of the Faure sequence in 3 dimensions. We plot the first component at the top, the second component in the middle and the third component at the bottom. We see that all the points in each coordinate are at the same positions, but come in a different order. This point set is made, indeed, of permuted point sets from the Van Der Corput sequence in base b=3.

We can also look at the distribution of sub-sequences of the Faure point set in s=3 dimensions. In the following script, we consider a set of 3^4=81 points, organized in 3 consecutive blocks of 3^3=27 points. We plot the first component at the top, the second component in the middle and the third component at the bottom. The goal of this experiment is to visualize the point sets ib^r,...,(i+1)b^r-1, for i=0,1,...,b-1, with r=2. We can see these particular sub-sequences of consecutive 27 points are evenly filling the one dimensionnal interval [0,1], so that the sub-sequences are translated by 1/27 each time a new block i is considered.
